name: HMS P 36 (P 36)
events: The history of HMS P 36 as compiled on this page is extracted from P 36's patrol reports and created in October 2014. [unfortunately no logbooks of this submarine are available.]
Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in April 2026.
23 Sep 1941 P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)
24 Sep 1941 Around 1515 A/24, P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow to begin a training period.
En-route from Barrow acceptance trials had been carried out. On arrival at Holy Loch she was taken over from the shipyard and commissioned in full.
[No details for the work-up period can be given as unfortunately no logbooks of HMS P 36 are available.] (1)

14 Oct 1941 Around 1250 A/14, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the North Atlantic to the south-west of Ireland on an anti-uboat patrol.
During passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN).
[As no logs are available for this submarine no map can be displayed. Also no patrol report is available for this patrol.] (2)
15 Oct 1941 During passage south through the Irish Sea, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) developed engine defects. She was ordered to return to Holy Loch. (1)
16 Oct 1941 Around 1635 A/16, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 1st (aborted) war patrol at Dundee. (2)

24 Oct 1941 Around 1800 A/24, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the North Atlantic to the south-west of Ireland on an anti-uboat patrol.
During passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until 2000 A/26 when they parted company in position 342° - Bishop Rock - 7 miles.

After parting company with the escort, P 36 was to proceed dived by day through position 50°00'N, 15°00'W to patrol in an area enclosed by latitudes 50°00'N and 51°00'N and longitudes 15°00'W and 17°00'W.
P 36 was to leave patrol area after dark on 2 November and proceed according to the orders that will be signalled by the Capt. S.5.
[No logs are available for this submarine no map can be displayed.] (3)

28 Oct 1941 At 0645 A/28, to the south of Ireland, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sighted seven white lights in the vicinity of the Great Sole Bank. These were subsequently identified as being lights of fishing vessels. (4)
29 Oct 1941 At 2300 A/29, P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in her patrol area. (4)

30 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1257 A/30, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); To avoid movements of own shipping proceed forthwith submerged by day to patrol in new area bounded by 49°00'N / 50°00'N and 12°00'W / 14°00'W. Bombing restrictions are arranged 25 miles either side of line joining 50°00'N, 15°00'W and 49°30'N, 14°00'W forthwith until 2559 A/31 and in new patrol area forthwith until 2359 A/2.
HMS P 36 received this signal at 1618 A/2 and after decoding the signal course was altered at 1630 A/2 to comply with these orders.
She arrived in her new patrol area at 0400 A/31. (5)

31 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1256 A/31, the C-in-C Plymouth, had sent the following orders to the escort destroyers ORP Kujawiak (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski) and HMS Atherstone (Lt.Cdr. R.F. Jenks, RN); Attack enemy submarine reported by aircraft at 1228 A/31 in position 48°05'N, 10°58'W, course 270°, speed 6 knots.
The aircraft, Hudson 'D' from 53 Sq. RAF, apparently also attacked the U-boat which was U-201.

In a signal timed 1304 A/31, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following information to HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); An aircraft with call sign H.K.6.D. reported at 1228 A/31 that she was over an enemy submarine in position 48°05'N, 10°58'W. Enemy course 270°, speed 6 knots.

In a signal timed 1432 A/31, the C-in-C Plymouth sent the following info to ORP Kujawiak and HMS Atherstone; My 1256 A/31. U-boat was attacked by aircraft, one hit. One Catalina and one Whitley coming to co-operate.
In a signal timed 1440 A/31, the C-in-C Plymouth sent the following info to ORP Kujawiak and HMS Atherstone; You were reported by enemy aircraft at 1312 A/31.
In a signal timed 1524 A/31, F.O.S. sent the following info the HMS P 36; My 1304 A/31. Two destroyers have been sent to attack enemy submarine.

In a signal timed 1652 A/31, The C-in-C Plymouth sent the following orders to ORP Kujawiak and HMS Atherstone; 1) If not in contact abandon hunt in time to pass through position 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles at 0800 A/1. Thence HMS Atherstone to Plymouth and ORP Kujawiak to Milford Haven. 2) Fighter protection arranged from 0800 A/1.

Attack report by the aircraft, Hudson 'D' from 53 Sq. RAF; Time: 1220 A/31. Position: 48°05'N, 10°58'W. Depth of water: 1000 fathoms. Weather conditions: wind 325°, 24 knots, Weather good, Visibility 12 to 15 miles.

While flying at 800 feet a U-boat was sighted on the surface at a range of 3 miles. Its course was 270° and speed about 6 knots. Commenced attack at once and the U-boat started to submerge at a range of about 3 miles. At the time of dropping depth charges, it was only just below the surface. Three 250lb. depth charges were dropped, the first set to 24 feet and the second and third to 50 feet. The attack was made across the U-boats track and the depth charges fell spaced 30 feet apart on the U-boats port beam, the first and nearest estimated as 20 feet from the U-boats hull. It is thought that the first and second depth charge exploded together. The aircraft then made a tight turn to port and released a 250lb. A/S bomb about 30 feet ahead of the swirl. The U-boat could be observed just beneath the surface and it is though that the bomb made a direct hit on the bows. Oil was thrown up to the surface when the bomb exploded and two minutes afterwards dark brown oil patches appeared which could be seen for 10 minutes. The aircraft remained in the vicinity for about 20 minutes and returned to the area after 40 minutes when three oil patches were seen, the largest of which was about 40 yards long and 25 yards wide.
Submarine tracking room's opinion; There is no tracking evidence indicative of the effect of this attack.

Decision of the U-boat assessment committee; The depth charge attack failed to straddle the U-boat and the nearest explosion would have been too far distant to inflict damage. If the fact of the bomb attack are as stated the bombs should have detonated fairly close to the U-boat. In view, however, of the lack of any evidence to show that any damage was inflicted the result is assessed as 'insufficient evidence of damage', the committee reckon that the three oil patches subsequently seen were the 'strains' of the depth charge attack.

Report of U-201;No position given but position at 1200 B/31 was grid BE 6364, which was centred in position 47°51'N, 11°55'W [Note the one degree difference in longitude !] 1312 B/31 (Note the one hour time zone difference), Crash dived for an aircraft on bearing 350° coming out of the low clouds. The aircraft dropped three bombs followed by a depth charge. Only some minor damage was sustained.
1536 B/31, Surfaced and continued passage. (6)

1 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1600 A/1, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Leave patrol after dark on 2 November diving by day through position 49°30'N, 12°00'E routed as follows; A) 180° - Bishop Rock - 8 miles B) 180° - Lizard Head - 15 miles C) 50°13'N, 01°18'W D) 50°23'N, 00°29'W to rendezvous with escorts in position 50°31'N, 00°41'W at 0730 A/6.

Submerged bombing restrictions arranged as follows; 1) 20 miles either side of a line joining position 49/30'N, 12.00'W and position 'A' (180° - Bishop Rock - 8 miles) from 1700 A/2 to 2359 A/4. 2) In area 51 east of 05°30'W and south of 50°00'N from 0001 A/4 to 1900 A/5. 3) In area 53 from 1200 A/4 to 0800 A/6. 4) In area 41 from 0001 A/5 to 1030 A/6. Bishop Rock light wil be exhibited for five minutes every half hour from 0200 A/4 to 0405 A/4. (7)

2 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1107 A/2, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Cancel my 1600 A/1 except paragraphs 3 and 4 of the bombing restrictions. Leave Patrol after dark/2 diving by day routed as follows [route not included in the signal but position 47°30'N, 10°00'W seemed to have been one of the position she was to pass through] to R/V in position 50°31'N 00°41'W at 0730 A/6. (7)
7 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Gosport (Portsmouth). The patrol had been uneventful.
She was escorted in (identity of the escort(s) unknown. The escort(s) had joined around 0930 A/7. (4)
21 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Gosport (Portsmouth) to participate in A/S exercises off Dartmouth.

She was to proceed via Q.Z.S. 284 thence diving by day through the following positions; (A) 50°31'N, 02°01'W. (B) 50°19'N, 02°11'W. (C) 130° - Start Point - 14 miles to R/V with two ML's in position 110° - Start Point - 6 miles at 0900 A/22. P 36 was to remain east of position 'C' (130° - Start Point - 14 miles) until daylight. (8)
22 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived at Dartmouth. The scheduled A/S exercises for this day were cancelled owing to the unsuitable weather conditions. (1)

23 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Dartmouth for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay (area, Bay of Biscay North Zone) and to perform a special operation.
On departure A/S exercises were carried out with HMS Brocklesby (Lt.Cdr. G.P. Huddart, RN), ML 157 (?), ML 159 (?) and ML 160 (?).
HMS P 36 was to proceed through her patrol area by passing through the following positions; 180° - Start Point - 15 miles, 48°45'N, 06°00'W and 47°48'N, 06°00'W.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (4)

25 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1701 A/25, D.D.I.C. [Deputy Director Intelligence Centre], informed HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) of the following; U-boats have been reported by aircraft in the following positions; 1) 47°15'N, 09°20'W, Course 100° at 1346 A/25 [This is probably a typo and most likely 1246 A/25 was intended]. 2) 47°37'N, 08°11'W, Course 110° at 1505 A/25. 3) 48°17'N, 08°13'W, Course 230° at 1501 A/25.
1) Referred to U-552 which was sighted and attacked by a Hudson (aircraft 'A') of 53 Squadron RAF.
2) This also referred to U-552 which was again sighted and attacked by the same Hudson that had attacked her earlier in the day.
3) Referred to U-558 which was sighted and attacked by a Hudson (aircraft 'T') of 233 Squadron RAF.
Neither submarine had sustained damage in these attacks. (9)
26 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) attacked a German U-boat with two torpedoes south-west of Belle-Ile island in position 47°16'N, 03°20'W. All torpedoes missed their target.
This must have been U-67 which had sailed from Lorient for an Atlantic patrol. [No other U-boats sailed or arrived at Lorient on this day.] The attack was not noticed by the Germans.
1529 A/26 - Heard HE bearing 020°.
1530 A/26 - Sighted a U-boat on that bearing. Enemy course was 180°, speed 14 knots. Started attack.

1543 A/26 - Fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards. Only two torpedoes were fired as there was no torpedo in No.1 tube due to routines being carried out and No.3 tube was not flooded in time. Both torpedoes were heard to run but no explosions followed. (4)

27 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) conducted a special operation. In the evening four agents were taken off a French fishing vessel and stores were transferred to this fishing vessel. This was Special Operation 'Fighter' or 'Valise III' for the SIS (Secret Intelligence Service).
1700 A/26 - Departure was taken from Belle Ile and during the night of 26/27 November, bearings of Ile de Groix and Penmarch were obtained.
0730 A/27 - Dived in position 320°, 12 miles from the rendezvous position.

1155 A/27 - When about 5 miles to the north of the rendezvous position, sighted a fishing vessel bearing 015°. On closing to investigate this proved to be a ketch close hauled on the port tack, steering west-south-west. Lt. Edmonds considered this was possible a substitute for the expected fishing vessel Vincent Mitchelle beating up to the rendezvous. However Lt. Edmonds decided to make the rendezvous first and then return to the ketch for a closer look if no other vessel had been seen.
1500 A/27 - In position 47°21'N, 04°19'W. Proceeded to the south.
1502 A/27 - Received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1426 A/27.
1600 A/27 - Periscope visibility was reduced to about two miles by rain.
1603 A/27 - Surfaced for two minutes for an all round look. Nothing was sighted. Set course to the north-west to look for the ketch again.
1815 A/27 - Surfaced and continued to search for the ketch. Visibility was three miles.

2000 A/27 - Visibility increased to six miles and the weather had improved a little. Lt. Edmonds decided to attempt to make contact with the fishing vessel before 2300 A/27 and complete the operation while the weather was suitable.

2239 A/27 - Sighted a single white light for 5 minutes and again at 2300 A/27. Approached it down wind. At about 3 cables it was identified as a vessel similar in appearance to the Vincent Mitchelle showing a white light up the mainmast. As the sea was too rough to use the Folbot, the fishing vessel was approached to within hailing distance and M. Daniel asked her number, to which she replied correctly. For her appearance Lt. Edmonds was satisfied she was harmless.

The fishing vessel turned out to be the Veach Vad and she attempted to come alongside but was unable to do so. The four agents were subsequently transferred, two at a time, in a small dinghy, crossing the lee of HMS P 36.
It was a hazardous operation for at least one of the passengers, but they all boarded the submarine drier than expected. Some stores were transferred to the fishing vessel.
0100 A/28 - The operation was completed and P 36 proceeded to resume patrol. (4)
28 Nov 1941 Upon completion of the special operation, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) resumed patrol near Belle-Ile island.
At 1005 A/28, an unarmed trawler was seen 8 miles to the south-west of Belle-Ile island proceeding northwards. As the trawler was unarmed this was most likely a fishing vessel.

At 1825 A/28, HMS P 36 surfaced and left patrol to return to Dartmouth. She was to return through positions 47°48'N, 06°00'W and 48°45'N, 06°00'W to make rendezvous with the escort in position 110° - Start Point - 3 miles [perhaps a typo and 5 miles was intended]. This in accordance with the Capt. S.5's signal timed 1701 A/28 which had contained the following orders; Make rendezvous with HMML 155 and HMML 160 in position 110° - Start Point - 5 miles at 0900 A/30. Bombing restrictions arranged between latitude 049°30'N and 50°49'N and longitude 03°00'W and 04°20'W from 0001 A/30 to 1700 A/30. (4)

28 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1501 A/28, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was informed of the following by the Capt. S.5; Bombing restrictions arranged as follows; l) Area 52 east of 06°30'W from 1800 A/28 to 1200 A/30. 2) Area 53 from 0500 A/29 to 1200 A/30. (7)
30 Nov 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Dartmouth. (4)

1 Dec 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Dartmouth with HMS Cleveland (Cdr. G.B. Sayer, RN), HMS Coriolanus (T/S.Lt. I.R. Saddler, RNVR), HMS Pearl (T/Lt. G.K. Hill, RNVR) and HMS Lord Essenden (Skr. J.T. Denoon, RNR).
HMS P 36 also carried out gunnery exercises on a target that was being towed by HMS Mangrove (T/Lt. J.K.M. Warde, RNVR) (10)
3 Dec 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) carried out A/S exercises off Dartmouth with aircraft. (10)
4 Dec 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Dartmouth for Gosport (Portsmouth).
She was to proceed through positions 50°05'N, 02°00'W and 50°12'N, 01°18'W to make rendezvous with the escort in position 50°31'N, 00°41'W at 0815 A/5. (1)
5 Dec 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived at Gosport (Portsmouth) from Dartmouth. (1)
9 Dec 1941 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) is docked at Portsmouth. Most likely this was at Gosport (Fort Blockhouse / HMS Dolphin) in AFD 2.
[It is currently not known to us when she was undocked.] (11)

18 Dec 1941 At 1345 A/18, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Gosport (Portsmouth) for passage to Gibraltar It had originally been intended for her to patrol in the Bay of Biscay and then to proceed to Gibraltar upon completion of the patrol but these orders were cancelled and she was ordered to proceed to Gibraltar directly.

She was to proceed through positions 50°31'N, 02°01'W, 50°19'N, 02°11'W, 49°00'N, 06°00'W thence to position 'F' (43°00'N, 12°15'W). HMS P 36 was to remain east of 05°00'W until 2000 A/19. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).
HMS P 36 was escorted out until 1705 A/18, by HMS Kingston Topaz (Skr. W.M. Smith, RNR).
P 36 was to join the 10th submarine flotilla at Malta. (12)
18 Dec 1941 For the daily positions of HMS P 36 during the passage from Gosport (Portsmouth) to Gibraltar see the map below.

19 Dec 1941 At 1300 A/19, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the following orders from the Capt. S.5 in his signal timed 1116 A/19; Adjust route to pass through position 49°00'N, 07°06'W and proceed onward "by route E', thence patrol in 'position 10' (44°40'N, 11°00'W). Remain to eastward of longtitude 05°00'W until 2000 A/19th and be clear either to eastward or westward of area between 05°30'W and 06°30'W at 0800 A/20th punctually. (13)

21 Dec 1941 At 0013 A/21, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2144 A/20 in which her patrol was cancelled and she was ordered to proceed direct to Gibraltar.

At 1622 A/21, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 1410 A/21 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 36; Report position, course and speed of advance on crossing 43°00'N. Proceed through positions 'P' (43°00'N, 11°13'E_ and 'R' (36°40'N, 10°25'E, thence to position 'Z' (350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles). Proceed on the surface at your discretion. (13)

23 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0014 A/23 [or 0114 A/23], HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sent the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); My position, course and speed. 43°00'N, 11°13'W, 175°, 9.5 knots.

In a reply timed 1336 A/23, HMS P 36 received the following orders; Your 0014 A/23 [or 0114 A/23] acknowledged. 1) Dive by day on the 25th and 26th. Adjust speed to pass through position 37°00'N, 10°25'W at 0730 A/25. 2) Rendezvous with the escort in position 'Z' (350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles) at 0830 A/27. 3) Bombing restrictions instituted assuming you will dive between 37°00'N, 10°25'W and 36°40'N, 10°10'W on the 25th also between 36°15'N, 08°15'W and 36°10'N, 07°35'W on the 26th. 4) An A/S trawler is on patrol inside 40 miles from Cape da Roca. (14)

24 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1606 A/24, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; HMS Vanoc (A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN) off Cape Ortegal at 1228 A/24th has been ordered to Gibraltar by shortest route at best speed. She has been given your movements. (14)
27 Dec 1941 At 0810 A/27, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) made rendezvous in position 350 - Cape Spartel - 5 miles with HMS Hesperus (Lt.Cdr. A.A. Tait, RN).
At 1230 A/27, HMS P 36 arrived at Gibraltar.
HMS Hesperus only entered harbour later as she joined ships exercising off Gibraltar. (15)

1 Jan 1942 At 1400 A/1, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 4th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Lions and to conduct two special operations (2 agents were to be landed). Upon completion of the patrol she was to proceed to Malta.
She was to proceed through position 36°30'N, 02°00'W and to keep 15 miles off the Spanish coast up to the vicinity of Sète, southern France.

For the daily positions of HMS P 36 during this patrol see the map below. No position is known for 14 January 1942. [Positions are taken from the patrol report as no logbooks are available for HMS P 36.]
5 Jan 1942 At 0830 A/5, near the Gulf of Rosas, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sighted a medium sized merchant vessel proceeding southward inside Spanish territorial waters.
At 1315 A/5, near the Spanish / French border a small Portugese coaster was seen making for Cape San Sebastian from the east-north-east. (16)

7 Jan 1942 At 2130 A/7, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) abandoned the landing of agent 'A' due to the unfavourable weather condition and the time limit now having expired. Course was subsequently set to the vicinity of Cannes. (16)
9 Jan 1942 At 1920 A/9, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) landed agent 'B' (Peter Churchill) from position 43°08'5"N, 06°56'8"E.

At 2100 A/9, agent 'A' was landed from position 43°06'N, 06°54'8"E. Agent 'A' was keen on being landed instead of returning and after consulting with agent 'B' it had been decided to land him in this location.
Course was then set to proceed to position 38°30'N, 09°00'E via the 'Free for all' area to the west of Sardinia. (16)
16 Jan 1942 At 0800 B/16, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

22 Jan 1942 At 2300 B/22, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the East of Tripoli in position 32°50'N, 14°23'E to intercept an important enemy convoy.

The convoy referred to was made up of the transports Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940), Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941), Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) and Victoria (Italian, 13098 GRT, built 1931) en-route from Italy to Tripoli (Ravello returned to Messina due to defects). They were escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Antonio da Noli, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the torpedo boats Orsa and Castore.

A close cover force for this convoy was made up of the light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta, Muzio Attendolo and Raimondo Montecuccoli and the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Carabiniere and Fuciliere.
A distant cover force for this convoy was made up of the battleship Duilio and the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Alfredo Oriani, Scirocco and Ascari.

This was Operation T 18: The Italian 7th Cruiser Squadron (Duca d’Aosta, etc. under Admiral De Courten) sailed from Taranto at 0947 B/22-1112 B/22 and returned at 1520 B/25-1625 B/25. It was unsuccessfully attacked by HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO, RN) at 1347 C/22 (Italians: 1450 B/22) and joined the convoy at 2142 B/23 (probably to reinforce the AA defence after the air attack), The Duilio squadron (under Admiral Bergamini) sailed from Taranto screening Victoria at 1545 B/22-1624 B/22 and returned at 0610 B/25-0800 B/25 (except Ascari which picked up survivors of Victoria and went to Tripoli).

They were actually two convoys: (1) Monviso, Vettor Pisani and Ravello escorted by the destroyers Carabiniere, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Antonio Da Noli, Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello and the torpedo boat Castore sailed from Messina at 0700 B/22-0800 B/22 (the torpedo Orsa had sailed from Naples at 1735 B/21 to join the convoy, both torpedo boats were equipped with the German S-gerat (sonar), for Tripoli where they arrived at 1430 B/24-1715 B/24. Ravello had steering gear defects and had to turn back shortly after sailing. (2) The liner (troop transport) Victoria sailed with the Duilio squadron.

The two convoys merged at 1800 B/22. At 1730 B/23, three RAF torpedo bombers attacked and Victoria was hit astern on the starboard side, she was hit by two more torpedoes at 1840 B/23 and 1850 B/23 and sank at 1910 B/23, 1064 survivors were picked up by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera and Ascari of the 1455 on board and arrived at Tripoli at 1602 B/24-1630 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli at 1841 B/23-1845 B/23 to reinforce the escort of the convoy.
At 0735 B/24, the Seventh Cruiser Squadron left the convoy to return to Taranto.
At 1440 B/24, the Duilio squadron left the convoy to return to Taranto.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (16)

23 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 0203 B/23, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) that a battleship, a liner, three destroyers and two unknown vessels proceeding on course 180° at 15 knots had been sighted of Cape Colleone at 0105 B/23.
In a signal timed 1725 B/23 the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Urge and HMS P 36 to proceed with all dispatch by day, estimated enemy will pass your positions around 0200 B/24.
In a signal timed 1740 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; Aircraft will attack with bombs and torpedoes about midnight. No friendly surface force will be near you.

In a signal timed 2010 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; one battleship, three cruisers, sixteen destroyers and four merchant vessels were seen at 1740 B/23 in position 33°34'N, 16°52'E proceeding on course 240° at 15 knots.
In a signal timed 2337 B/23 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge and HMS P 36 of the following; Position, course and speed of the convoy at 2020 B/23 was 33°15'N, 16°45'E, 187°, 14 knots. (17)
24 Jan 1942 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) attacked an enemy convoy north of Khoms, Libya. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.
[For the composition of the convoy see the event for 22 December 1941].
Following the attack HMS P 36 was hunted by the Italian destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Geniere, the Italian torpedo boat Castore and a CANT Z.501 of 196^ Squadriglia.
0535 B/24 - Sighted flares bearing 100°. Assumed this to be aircraft attacking the expected convoy.
0704 B/24 - Dived in position 32°53'N, 14°21'E.
0720 B/24 - Commenced patrol at periscope depth.
0810 B/24 - Heard Asdic transmissions and HE on bearing 130°.

0824 B/24 - In position 32°50'N, 14°20'E sighted the masts of three merchant vessels, later seen to be of 5000 tons each. They were escorted by 5 destroyers and 2 aircraft. Started attack. Closed at high speed to 4500 yards.

0859 B/24 - Fired four torpedoes at the two leading merchant vessels which were a length apart. It is thought that one hit was obtained. Went to 130 feet on firing and moved away on an opposite course to the convoy. The counter attack was carried out by four destroyers. About 30 depth charges were dropped but none were very close.
1010 B/24 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1030 B/24 - Went to 70 feet to reload.

1123 B/24 - Heard a destroyer's HE bearing 120°. Two tubes had meanwhile been reloaded and P 36 returned to periscope depth hoping to see the remainder of the convoy but only two destroyers were seen, out of range to the southward, steering west.
1200 B/24 - Went to 70 feet again to reload the two other torpedo tubes.
1245 B/24 - Returned to periscope depth on completion of the reload.
1940 B/24 - Surfaced.

2210 B/24 - Proceeded to patrol in area 'H', south of 35°30'N and west of 12°40'E (this was an area between Kerkennah and Lampedusa). En-route P 36 was to proceed through positions 33°20'N, 13°40'E and 33°20'N, 12°50'E. (16)

25 Jan 1942 At 2210 B/25, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1209 B/25 which advised her to patrol between positions 33°06'N, 12°55'E and 33°09'N, 12°05'E (This was along the coastal route to the west of Tripoli). Also this signal reported the expected movement of westbound traffic P.M. today.
P 36 proceeded at 10 knots to the most westerly position to intercept any ship leaving Tripoli at dusk.

The positioning of P 36 was probably due to an ULTRA intercept [see file DEFE3/837/pp. 968-970] which expected the transport Napoli (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) to sail from Tripoli at 1600 B/25 for Palermo escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Orsa. They actually sailed at 1510 B/25, but at 1600 B/25, they received an order from Marilibia to return immediately and they arrived in Tripoli at 1840 B/25. They finally sailed at 1700 B/29 and arrived in Trapani at 1700 B/2 (February). (16)

27 Jan 1942 At 1034 B/27, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0951 B/27 which contained the following information; One merchant ship and one escort in position 34°23'N, 11°47'E, course 160°, speed 5 knots at 0913 B/27.
P 36, which was still patrolling to the west of Tripoli, set course to the north, proceeding submerged at 4 knots, to intercept before dark.

At 1304 B/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1141 B/27 was received which contained the following; My 0951 B/27. Correct position 33°21'N, 11°40'E at 1025 B/27. Aircraft are leaving Malta now to attack.
HMS P 36 altered course to intercept but nothing was sighted.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Ariosto (4115 GRT, built 1902) which was on passage from Trapani to Tripoli with a short stop at Pantelleria. She was initially escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cantore.
This convoy had sailed from Trapani at 1035 B/25-1150 B/25. They stopped at Pantelleria (2200 B/25-0630 B/26) and arrived in Tripoli via Kuriat at 1535 B/27-1815 B/27.

[The Italian torpedo boat Cigno, which is also listed as escort for part of the way in the Italian Official History, did not take part in the escort, she was at Palermo at the time. This is unfortunately an error in the Italian Official History.] (18)
28 Jan 1942 At 0650 B/28, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) dived and patrol to the north-north-west of position 33°16'N, 11°46'E.

At 1538 B/28, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1340 B/28 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol vicinity of position 34°46'N, 11°50'E from 0700 B/29 where southbound ships may be expected during daylight and northbound after dark.

This expected southbound traffic referred to the following convoy; German transport Bengasi (former French Saint Philippe, 1567 GRT, built 1933) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Clio. They departed Palermo at 1949 B/28 and arrived at Tripoli at 0837 B/30.

This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 1949 B/28. They were expected to reach position 34°24'N, 12°20'E at 2000 B/29, but were about an hour and a half behind schedule. They were then to pass through position 33°48'N, 13°57'E. At 0010 B/30, they were attacked by three Swordfish torpedo bombers of 830 Squadron (FAA) in position 34°02'N, 12°38'E (a fourth A.S.V. equipped Swordfish also participated in the search). They managed to avoid them (although the Swordfish pilots claimed a direct hit) with Bengasi shooting down one of them, this was aircraft 'A' piloted by S.Lt. Wilson. At 0610 B/30, they sighted the torpedo boat Cantore which had sailed from Tripoli at 0335 B/30 to pilot them in. They arrived in destination at 0830 B/30-037B/30. The sailing of Bengasi (mistaken for the Italian transport Tembien (5584 GRT, built 1914)) was known through ULTRA [See file DEFE3/837/pp. 792-792A].

The northbound traffic most probably referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) which departed Tripoli on 28 January for Italy but had had broken her propeller’s axle and had to drop anchor off Mahedia, Tunisia. Because of the heavy seas, attempts by her escort, the Italian torpedo boat Orsa to tow her failed. On 2 February, Orsa was relieved by the Italian torpedo boat Procione and the naval auxiliary (cable layer) Giasone (630 tons, built 1941). In an attack on 3 February by HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) Napoli was hit astern and damaged, five of the crew were wounded by the explosion. Giasone took her in tow towards Sousse but after a few miles, she had to be beached the following day in 35°27'N, 11°03'E. Napoli was destroyed by aircraft on the 11th.

It was known through ULTRA, that the transports Napoli and Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912) were expected to sail from Tripoli on the 28th [file DEFE3/837/pp. 773-774]. However, a new signal was intercepted stating that the sailings were cancelled [file DEFE3/837/p. 768] and another that they would be ready to sail on Thursday the 29th [file DEFE3/837/p. 755], Napoli to be escorted by the torpedo boat Orsa and Beppe by the destroyer Antonio da Noli for Palermo with Napoli continuing to Naples.

Beppe had been torpedoed by HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) on 18 October 1941 and after being patched up was now ready to sail for home. Another signal of 29 January indicated that the departure of Beppe had been delayed by 24 hours [file DEFE3/837/pp. 724, 703, 660, 424]. She eventually departed from Tripoli unescorted at 1500 B/22 (February !) and arrived in Palermo at 0330 B/26 (February !). The departure of the destroyer Antonio da Noli was also delayed. After briefly sent on 31st January to assist the steamer San Giovanni Battista (Italian, 5628 GRT, built 1913), torpedoed by enemy aircraft near Tripoli, she was now to escort the motor vessel Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), and they would eventually sail from Tripoli with the additional escort of the torpedo boat Castore at 1717 B/6 February and arrive at Trapani at 0145 B/8.

Napoli escorted by Orsa did sail from Tripoli at 1600 B/29-1700 B/29. At 1425 B/30, off Kuriat, in bad weather, Napoli signalled to Orsa that the propeller shaft was giving her problems and that she should try to reach Sousse (Tunisia) when the weather improved. Orsa signalled back that they would immediately make for Sousse, but, at 1455 B/30, the freighter signalled that the shaft was broken and she would require towing. The torpedo boat took her in tow, and they proceeded northward. At 1150 B/31, they were joined by the refrigeration ship Ascianghi (Italian, 610 GRT, built 1921), arriving from Hammamet, which followed them in case they needed assistance. At 1505 B/31, Napoli dropped anchor in 130° - Mahedia Light – 6 miles, and Ascianghi was given freedom of action. Supermarina ordered the torpedo boat to patrol outside the bay, and that the cable vessel Giasone (Italian, 630 tons, built 1941) would sail from Trapani to assist with the towing. She sailed at 2030 B/31 but at 2200 B/1, Supermarina informed them that Giasone had turned back to Trapani due to the bad weather conditions. At 0400 B/2, Orsa, short on fuel, proceeded to Trapani where she arrived in 1700 B/2. The torpedo boat Procione sailed from Trapani at 2345 B/2. At 1700 B/3, she sighted a submarine 10 miles east of Cape Africa (Mahedia) and hunted it [HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN)]. On 3 February, Napoli was torpedoed by HMS P 35, and beached near Mahedia to avoid sinking, she was a total loss. Procione finally entered the bay of Mahedia at 1900 B/4. At 1930 B/4, she sailed from there escorting Giasone, and they reached Trapani at 1000 B/5.
At 1945 B/28, HMS P 36 surfaced and proceeded towards the newly ordered patrol position. (18)

29 Jan 1942 At 0530 B/29, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in the newly ordered patrol position (34°46'N, 11°50'E). It was not possible to conduct periscope depth patrol between 1300 B/29 and 1745 B/29 owing to the weather conditions.

In a signal timed 1211 B/29, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 36; Leave patrol through positions 35°00'N, 12°01'E and 35°34'N, 14°12'E and by the south-east searched channel to arrive at Malta on 2 February. You are not to attack any submarine encountered after dark within 15 miles of Filfola Island.

At 2207 B/29, a report from an aircraft was received that at 2045 B/29 and 2110 B/29 a merchant vessel and a destroyer had been sighted in position 150° - 40 miles from P 36. The enemy was steering 130° at 11 knots.
At 2258 B/29, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2223 B/29 was received in which it was stated that aircraft were taking off from Malta to attack the reported merchant vessel.

At 2345 B/29, flares were sighted bearing 030°. Lt. Edmonds considered this to be the aircraft attacking the merchant vessel who was thus to the northward of her reported position. In this case there would be a chance of intercepting the enemy and P 36 altered course to 130° and increased speed to 10 knots. (18)

30 Jan 1942 At 0301 B/30, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0150 B/30 which contained the following; My 2223 B/29. One hit obtained in posiution 34°00'N, 12°36'E. Search vicinity of position 34°10'N, 12°40'E for dinghy containing survivors of one Swordfish aircraft crashed at 0100 B/30. Exercise great caution when near latter position [this was the Swordfish shot down by the German transport Bengasi (former French Saint Philippe, 1567 GRT, built 1933), see the entry for 28 January 1942].
At 0300 B/30, HMS P 36 altered course to 090° for one hour and then searched in a north-west direction but nothing was sighted.
At 1030 B/30, HMS P 36 proceeded deep at 4 knots to proceed to the reported position in which a search for the Swordfish crew was to be undertaken.
At 1947 B/30, HMS P 36 surfaced. The wind was north-west, force 5 and in spite of a full moon there was little hope of finding the survivors.

At 2005 B/30, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1833 B/30 was received which contained the following orders; Before complying with my 1211 B/29 endeavour to reach position in my 1340 B/28 by 0300 B/31 when a 14 knot northbound ship may be encountered which Swordfish will attack north of Kerkennah No.4 buoy.

On 28 January, an ULTRA intercept had revealed that the Italian transport Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Procione would be ready to sail from Tripoli on Saturday the 31st. [file DEFE3/837/p. 755]. At the end of the afternoon on 30 January, another intercept confirmed that Monginevro escorted by the torpedo boat Castore, instead of the Procione, had sailed [file DEFE3/837/p. 657]. In fact, the two ships had sailed from Tripoli at 1640 B/30-1700 B/30 at a speed of 15 knots, but this was progressively reduced to 6 knots due to the rough weather. At 2150 B/30, Castore signalled Marina Tripoli that she had to abandon the escort and received the authorisation at 2300 B, Monginevro continued alone and reached Naples at 1100 B/1 (February). At 1025 B/31, Castore anchored off the west coast of Kerkennah to wait for better weather. She finally arrived in Tripoli at 1317 B/1 (February).
At 2035 B/30, course was altered to return to the patrol position. (18)

31 Jan 1942 At 0600 B/31, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position (34°46'N, 11°50'E). The head wind and heavy sea had delayed HMS P 36 but would also have delayed the enemy convoy.
At 0629 B/31, an aircraft report timed 0545 B/31 was received which reported that the merchant vessel was laying stopped with a heavy list in position 150° - 60 miles from HMS P 36.

This aircraft report referred to the Italian transport San Giovanni Battista (5628 GRT, built 1913) which, escorted by the torpedo boat Calliope, had sailed from Tripoli at 1715 B/30-1800 B/30 for Trapani. The bad weather forced them to reduce speed to 9 knots. At 0040 B/31, a twin-engined aircraft flew over them, and they opened fire with their machine guns. At 0440 B/31, the freighter was hit by a torpedo amidship on the starboard side and took a list. A biplane circled them, and Calliope was firing her machine guns to keep it at bay. The attack was carried out by four Swordfish of 830 Squadron (FAA), one was an A.S.V. aircraft which dropped flares and the other three armed with torpedoes, they claimed two certain hit and a probable hit in position 34°55'N, 12°20'E (the freighter had only been hit once, Calliope gave the position as 33°45.5'N, 12°17'E). At 0530 B/31, the aircraft were gone, and the torpedo boat began to look for survivors. The heavy seas made the rescue very difficult. Survivors were clinging to a capsized lifeboat and Caliope picked them up, but those who were dispersed could not be rescued, and their cries faded away. Calliope proceeded to pick up those from two other lifeboats. There were still some crew members on board, and the torpedo boat could not get alongside because of the heavy waves. They were asked to abandon ship on a raft and were finally picked up at 0850 B/1 February. Calliope arrived in Tripoli at 1538 B/1 and landed 72 survivors; there were 54 killed or missing. The stricken vessel was now completely abandoned but was still floating. The destroyer Antonio da Noli sailed from Tripoli at 1500 B/31 and encountered the torpedo boat Calliope which was bringing the survivors. At 1837 B/31, she sighted the drifting San Giovanni Battista in position 33°42’ N, 12°34’ E and stood by, ready to cover her with a smoke screen in case of air attack. Enemy aircraft dropped flares but no attack developped. The tug Ciclope sailed from Tripoli at 0000 B/1 and had a bit of trouble locating them, the destroyer finally directed her to the freighter which was taken in tow at 0720 B/1. As they were getting close to Tripoli, the destroyer left them and entered harbour at 0504 B/2, San Giovanni Battista and Ciclope arrived in 0920 B/2.
As the wind had dropped to force 3 and the sky was clear Lt. Edmonds decided to close the disabled ship on the surface.
At 0925 B/31, HMS P 36 dived for an aircraft surfacing again at 1021 B/31 to continue the surface passage.

At 1025 B/31, HMS P 36 once more had to dive for an aircraft and it was decided to close the remaining 23 miles dived. P 36 went deep and proceeded at 4 knots coming to periscope depth to have a look around every hour.
From 1630 B/31 onwards HMS P 36 kept periscope depth patrol.
At 1940 B/31, HMS P 36 surfaced and continued on a southerly course.
At 2040 B/31, after nothing had been sighted course was set to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1211 B/29.

At 2243 B/31, flares and a short burst of tracer from gunfire was sighted bearing 150°. This was assumed to be the Swordfish attack. Course was altered and a search was commenced in a south-easterly direction.
At 2352 B/31, a Wellington aircraft was seen overhead.
At 0022 B/1, the search was discontinued and course was set, once more, to return to Malta. (18)
2 Feb 1942 At 0730 B/2, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

11 Feb 1942 At 1930 B/11, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of the Straits of Messina in a rectangle bounded by lines joining the following positions; 37°48'N, 15°36'E, 38°05'N, 16°14'E, 37°31'N, 16°39'E, 37°13'N, 16°02'E. A position, (37°43'N, 15°45'E) to the south-south-east of Cape dell'Armi had been chosen as the focus point.
[No logs are available for HMS P 36 so no map can be displayed.] (16)
13 Feb 1942 At 0200 B/13, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in the intended patrol position (37°43'N, 15°45'E). (16)

15 Feb 1942 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (3rd Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Ascari, Aviere, Geniere and Camicia Nera (11th Destroyer Division), south of the Straits of Messina in position 37°36'N, 15°53'E. These ships had sailed from Messina at 2205 B/14-2253 B/14.
Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. The destroyer Ascari reported that a torpedo missed her astern.
0055 B/15 - Heard HE bearing 295°. Stopped the generators.
0102 B/15 - HE was reported to be drawing from right to left. Closed the range, keeping the HE 10° - 15° on the starboard bow, varying speed between 9 and 3 knots.
0115 B/15 - Sighted two cruisers and one destroyer bearing 265°. The destroyer being on the engaged bow of the cruisers. Started attack.
0121 B/15 - Fired four torpedoes from 7000 yards at the leading cruiser. No hits were obtained.
0122 B/15 - Dived as the torpedo tracks were fairy conspicuous owing to the phosphorescence.
0220 B/15 - Surfaced to pass enemy report timed 0121 B/15 which stated the following; Two cruisers and one destroyer in position 37°39'N, 15°54'E, course 150° speed 20 knots.
0609 B/15 - Dived and reloaded all tubes. Proceeded to the north-west to close Cape Spartivento.
1958 B/15 - Surfaced.

2201 B/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2125 B/15 which stated the following; Three cruisers and nine destroyers were reported in position 35°08'N, 16°02'E, course 220°, speed 20 knots at 1245 B/15. These may be returning to Messina tonight.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) under Admiral Angelo Parona (former head of BETASOM, the Italian submarine base in Bordeaux), screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Ascari and Camicia Nera (11th Destroyer Squadron), had sailed from Messina at 2205-2317 B/14. The light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Eugenio Montecuccoli (Seventh Cruiser Division) under Divisional Admiral Raffaele de Courten, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Fuciliere and Carabiniere (13th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Taranto at 1830 B/14-2030 B/14. At 0800 B/15, the two squadrons joined. They were to intercept a convoy from Alexandria to Malta (convoy M.W. 9) and proceeded as far south as 34°00'N. At 0127 B/15, in position 37°37'N, 15°52'E, the destroyer Ascari sighted a torpedo which missed her astern; Trento reported hearing two underwater detonations. This was the attack by HMS P 36. The destroyer Alpino was detached and arrived in Augusta at 1450 B/15 and sailed again at 0420 B/16 to rejoin. At 0530 B/16, a torpedo bomber believed to be an Albacore [indeed this was an attack by Albacore torpedo bombers of 828 Squadron (FAA)] attacked the destroyer Geniere, in position 36°42'N, 18°16'E. The torpedo was avoided. Flares were dropped, and other torpedoes were also avoided in position 36°18'N, 18°10'E. At 0745 B/16, a Wellington bomber was shot down by a German aircraft in position 37°16'N, 18°08'E. The destroyer Fuciliere was detached to pick up the survivors and returned to Messina independently at 1615 B/16. At 1315 B/16, when just at the southern entrance of the Strait of Messina, the two heavy cruisers of Third Cruiser Squadron were proceeding in line abreast with the destroyer Alpino ahead, Carabiniere on the starboard flank and Bersagliere on the port flank. Gorizia sighted torpedo tracks, and the destroyer Carabiniere was struck by a torpedo on the starboard bow as the squadron turned hard to port, enabling Gorizia to avoid two torpedoes which passed about 10 metres astern. Carabiniere had 20 killed or missing and 40 wounded (3 seriously). This was again an attack by HMS P 36. A Junker 88 dropped a bomb at the origin of the torpedo track. Alpino and Bersagliere stayed behind to hunt the submarine and assist Carabiniere. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza, which had sailed from Augusta at 0500 B/16 for a transfer to Messina, was diverted and arrived on the scene at 1425 B/16 and took the destroyer in tow. They were joined by the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari and the tug Instancabile which had sailed from Messina at 1425 B/16 to assist. The motor torpedo boats MAS 547 and MAS 556 also sailed from Messina at 1600 B/16 to hunt the submarine and screen the crippled destroyer. They returned to Messina at 0037 B/17-0100 B/17. Instancabile took over the towing of Carabiniere at 1700 B/16. Alpino and Bersagliere left the scene and reached Messina at 1930 B/16-1940 B/16. Carabiniere towed by Instacabile and screened by the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Generale Marcello Prestinari arrived in Messina at 0915 B/17-1005 B/17. The torpedo boat Pallade, equipped with sonar, sailed from Messina at 1516 B/16 to hunt the submarine. At 1708 B/16, she sighted the submarine and, attacked with three patterns of fifteen depth-charges but without success at 1718, 1731 and 1749 hours. She could no longer get an echo and had expended all her depth charges. She observed debris and oil slicks, but since Carabiniere had lost part of her bow in this area, it was difficult to ascertain their origin. Supermarina assessed that the submarine had suffered at best slight damage. She returned to Messina at 1129 B/17. She had been relieved by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba, which had sailed from Messina at 2130 B/16 and used her towing mine to criss-cross the area and returned to Messina at 1840 B/17. She sailed again at 1455 B/18 to hunt the submarine again and returned at 1200 B/19. Gorizia and Trento returned to Messina at 1520 B/16-1600 B/16. The destroyer Fuciliere entered Messina at 1520 B/16. Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Eugenio Montecuccoli, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Ascari, Geniere and Camicia Nera were returning to Taranto when, at 0755 B/16, they were attacked by two torpedo bombers from the sun but avoided the torpedoes. One was shot down by an escorting German aircraft. They reached Taranto at 1743-2150 B/16. (16)
16 Feb 1942 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian destroyer Carabiniere south of the Straits of Messina. Part of the bow was blown off.
The destroyer was screening the heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia with her consorts Alpino, Fuciliere and Bersagliere.

Twenty men were killed or missing and forty wounded (three seriously). She was taken in tow by the tug Instancabile and, escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari, was brought to Messina. The torpedo boat Pallade was sailed to take over the hunt of the submarine and dropped forty-five depth charges.
0624 B/16 - Dived to the south of Cape dell'Armi in position 37°42'N, 15°37'E.
0944 B/16 - Sighted a Generale-class torpedo boat steering 110°. [Probably Giuseppe Dezza (see the entry of 15 February).]
1017 B/16 - Sighted two MA/SB's or MAS boats patrolling at slow speed.
This were probably MAS 556 and MAS 547 which had sailed from Messina at 0830 B/16 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1130 B/16. (See the entry of 15 February).

1030 B/16 - Rugby routine was missed while taking avoiding action for the MA/SB's and thus the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0903 B/16 ordering P 36 to position 37°55'N, 15°34'E to intercept four cruisers with a destroyer escort which were expected at noon. This signal was thus not received until after dark.
1100 B/16 - Went deep for 30 minutes to avoid the Generale-class torpedo boat which was seen returning. [Probably Giuseppe Dezza again (see the entry of 15 February).]
1215 B/16 - Went deep for an aircraft.
1240 B/16 - Heard destroyer HE bearing 110°.
1301 B/16 - At periscope depth. Sighted the Italian heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia. They were escorted by about eight destroyers and several aircraft. Started attack on the leading cruiser.
1307 B/16 - Shifted target to the second cruiser, Gorizia.
1315 B/16 - In position 37°42'N, 15°35'E fired four torpedoes from 1000 yards. It is thought two hits were obtained. P 36 went deep and took evasive action.
1318 B/16 - A counter attack started. This was carried out by six destroyers. During the first 45 minutes 105 depth charges were counted. None were uncomfortably close.
1322 B/16 - Altered to the firing course and crossed the track of the target while at 200 feet. All auxiliary motors were stopped.
1402 B/16 - No more HE was heard before the beam after this time but the destroyers continued to drop another 110 charges during the following 2.5 hours.
1534 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth. Three destroyers and two aircraft were sighted near the area of the attack about 3 nautical miles away. Nothing was seen of the cruiser.
2000 B/16 - Surfaced. Set course to the eastward.

2020 B/16 - Passed an enemy report timed 1315 B/16 which started the following; Two 8" cruisers and six destroyers in position 37°42'N, 15°35'E, course 300°, speed 25 knots. Estimated two hits one cruiser. All torpedoes expended.
2230 B/16 - Set course to 210° to leave the area and return to Malta. (16)

17 Feb 1942 At 0055 B/17, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2345 B/16 which contained the following orders; Well done. Return to Malta through positions 36°56'N, 15°30'E and 36°20'N, 15°20'E and by the north-east swept channel to arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) at 1930 B/17 or 0715 B/18. (19)
18 Feb 1942 At 0720 B/18, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

21 Feb 1942 At 1945 B/21, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Tripoli in a patrol line with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN) to intercept an important enemy convoy.

HMS P 36 was to patrol in position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E). En-route to this position she was to pass through position 34°10'N, 14°00'N about 2100 B/21 thence through position 32°48'N, 14°28'E to arrive in position 'W' at dawn/22.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).
At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron.
At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay.

At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23.

At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22.
At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24.

At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy.

Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23.

Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24.
The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24.
22 Feb 1942 At 1935 B/22, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) had to slow down due to a defect on the starboard engine. Speed had to be reduced to 3 knots. (16)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (20)
23 Feb 1942 In the evening HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) set course, as ordered, for a new patrol area to the West of Tripoli. (16)
23 Feb 1942 At 0345 B/23, on board HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), the repairs to the starboard engine were completed. Speed was now increased to 9.5 knots.
At 0725 B/23, HMS P 36 diced 25 north of position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E). Due to the engine defect it was not possible to be in her assigned patrol position at dawn.
From 0800 B/23 onwards, occasional distant depth charging was heard during the forenoon.
At 1000 B/23, two Italian destroyers, thought to be of the Freccia-class, were seen steering 290° at 20 knots.
This were probably Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno which had attacked HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and were rejoining the convoy.
At 1153 B/23, an Italian destroyer was seen bearing 210° at a range of 4 miles. The enemy appeared to be hunting.
This was probably the torpedo boat Circe which had just sunk HMS P 38.
At 1203 B/23, the destroyer increased speed and disappeared on a westerly course.
At 1600 B/23, HMS P 36 arrived in position 'W'. An oil track was sighted in the vicinity.
At 1957 B/23, HMS P 36 surfaced.
At 2100 B/23, HMS P 36 proceeded in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1207 B/23 ordering P 36 to patrol in area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. (16)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (20)

24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1251 B/24, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; Area intelligence. Area G probable enemy route 35°55'N, 12°10'E to 35°05'N, 12°55'E. Small sweeper usually encountered between Lampedusa and Lampion and M.A.3. may operate from former. Areas L and N , enemy route number 7 buoy Kerkennah 33°51'N, 11°10'E - 33°06'N, 12°00'E. Coasters may anchor off Turgeuness and Zuara at night. (20)
25 Feb 1942 At 0500 B/25, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in a position 28 miles - 090° from Ras Turgueness and commenced patrolling at slow speed in the area.
At 1301 B/25, a small ship of about 500 tons was sighted on bearing 063° at a range of about 3 miles. The vessel was steering towards Ras Turgueness.
At 1334 B/25, the ship altered course to 135° when still at a range of three miles.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (16)

27 Feb 1942 At 1950 B/27, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) surfaced and set course to proceed to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1240 B/25. She was ordered to proceed through positions 34°10'N, 12°30'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E to arrive at Malta on 1 March. (16)
1 Mar 1942 At 0645 B/1, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
6 Mar 1942 HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sustained damage to both her periscopes due to near misses during an air raid on the submarine base at Malta. (21)

14 Mar 1942 At 1945 B/14, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Taranto to provide cover for a convoy to Malta.

16 Mar 1942 At 0120 B/16, the Italian submarine Onice sighted a submarine at a distance of 800-1000 metres. Shortly after, Onice fired two torpedoes from her bow tubes at a range of 500 metres. She then opened fire as she steered straight for the enemy. After the third round, fired at only 150 metres, the submarine crash dived as Onice passed over her. The target was HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN). Position of the attack was 37°29'N, 16°32'E.
P 36 thought she had encountered a motor torpedo boat for which she dived.
At 0511 B/16, HMS P 36 sighted 'another' motor torpedo boat laying stopped for which she dived. (22)

16 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2039 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Concentration off Taranto postponed. Proceed as follows: Upholder through position 39°35'N, 18°34'E and 40°00'N, 18°55'E to patrol west of a line 350° through the last position and south of 04°50'N. P 36 patrol within 30 miles Colonne south of 39°15'N. Proteus through position 37°30'N, 20°30'E to patrol within 30 miles of cape Dukato west of 20°45'E concentrating on the Levkas Channel. A full bow salvo must be retained for use after arrival off Taranto probably P.M. on 20 March. (16)
17 Mar 1942 While patrolling off Cape Colonne, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sighted an auxiliary schooner patrolling in the area. (16)

18 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 0520 B/18, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) to patrol 20 miles to the east of Cape Colonne to intercept an enemy cruiser which might return to Taranto.

This referred to the light cruiser Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta screened by the destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Scirocco and Grecale which had sailed from Taranto at 1450 B/16-1903 B/16 to escort a convoy to Tripoli and were expected to turn back [see file DEFE3/839/1/pp. 626, 623, 612].

This convoy was formed in three sections (operation SIRIO); 1) The Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi which had sailed from Naples at 1904 B/14 and arrived in Messina at 1904 B/15. They sailed from Messina at 1330 B/16-1350 B/16, reinforced by the destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori. At 1706 B/16, Lanzerotto Malocello sighted a torpedo track in position 38°00'N, 16°11'E, near Cape Spartivento. (This was the attack by HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN). The escorting CANT Z.501 4/184^ Squadriglia sighted a torpedo fired from about 2000 meters and reported it and dropped two 160 kg bombs on the origin, but result was uncertain. Giuseppe Sirtori turned back during the evening and arrived in Messina at 0220 B/17. The remaining ships continued on to Tripoli.

2) The German transport Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936), escorted by the torpedo boats Lince and Polluce sailed from Naples at 1920 B/15-1935 B/15, but the latter had to turn back and arrived in Naples at 0042 B/16. As they were crossing the Strait of Messina, Reichenfels had a steering breakdown and stopped (0910 B/16-1640 B/16). Lince was relieved by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Pallade which had sailed from Messina at 0720 B/16. Lince returned to Naples at 2245 B/16.

3) The Italian transport Assunta di Gregori (4,219 GRT, built 1914), escorted by the destroyer Premuda which sailed from Palermo at 0158 B/16-0230 B/16 and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Castore which sailed from Trapani at 0800 B/16. They never united with the other two sections but followed the Tunisian route and arrived in Tripoli at 0712 B/19-0736 B/19.

At 0725 B/17, the Vettor Pisani and Reichenfels sections merged in position 37°05'N, 18°15'E with the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi assuming overall command. The Aosta squadron was sighted at 1050 B/17 in position 36°16'N, 18°17'E and escorted until position 33°47'20"N, 17°48'50"E. At 1800 B/17, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and the destroyers Grecale and Scirocco turned back, Nicolò Zeno however remained with the convoy. The cruiser and escort arrived in Taranto at 1825 B/18-2225 B/18 [see file DEFE3/839/1/pp. 626, 623, 612].

As the cruiser left the convoy, the latter split in two: 1) Reichenfels escorted by Emanuele Pessagno and Lanzerotto Malocello. 2) Vettor Pisani escorted by Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicolò Zeno and Pallade. At 0800 B/18, the two convoys reunited.

At 2200 B/17, the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the escort. At 0210 B/18, she located a lifeboat with 21 survivors of the German transport Achaia (1778 GRT, built 1922) and picked them up. She sighted the convoy at 0745 B/18 and joined at 0820 B/18.

[At 2000 B/17 March, the Achaia and the Italian transport Salona (former Yugoslav Hercegovina, 664 GRT, built 1913) escorted by the torpedo boat Calliope had sailed from Tripoli for Benghazi. The German ship was torpedoed by Swordfish from 830 Squadron (FAA) and then at 2345 B/17 drifted in an Italian defensive minefield and sank near Tripoli].
The convoy arrived in Tripoli at 1425 B/18-1600 B/18.

In addition, the following convoys were at sea but Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta though informed, never made contact with them [see file DEFE3/839/1/pp. 633-634, 627, 621, 609-610, 602, 596]: 1) The transport Reginaldo Giuliani (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the torpedo boat Perseo which sailed from Tripoli at 2030 B/17. The motor torpedo boats MAS 563 and MAS 564 temporarily joined the escort (1130 B/18-1321 B/18). The two ships arrived in Palermo at 2050 B/18-2240 B/18. 2) the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the torpedo boat Circe which sailed from Tripoli at 2110 B/17-2200 B/17 and arrived in Palermo at 2359 B/18.
Note: although the two convoys were to have merged, they never managed to join company. (23)

18 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1411 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); P 36, leave position 39°05'N, 17°38'E about 2000 B/19 to arrive in position 'A' (40°08'N, 17°08'E) at dawn/20. Proteus, leave area A.M./19 through position 39°30'N, 17°45'E about 0400 B/20 to arrive in position 'B' (40°00'N, 17°17'E) at noon/20. Upholder, leave as necessary by reverse of northward route and through position 39°30'N, 18°00'E about 0500 B/20 to arrive in position 'C' (39°50'N, 17°21'E) at dusk/20. Position 'D' will not be occupied. (23)
19 Mar 1942 At 1758 B/19, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) sighted what is thought the same auxiliary schooner that had been seen on the 17th patrolling once more off Cape Colonne.
At 2030 B/19, HMS P 36 surfaced after a day of submerged patrol. She then proceeded in accordance with the orders contained in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1411 B/18. (16)
20 Mar 1942 At 0530 A/20, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'A' (40°08'N, 17°08'E).
At 2030 A/20, P 36 surfaced. Nothing had been seen all day but during the early part of the night occasional searchlight and starshell were seen in the direction of Taranto. (16)
Operation MG 1, passage of convoy MW 10 to Malta.

Around 0700 B/20 convoy MW 10 departed Alexandria for Malta. This convoy was made up of the transports HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939), Clan Campbell (British, 7255 GRT, built 1937), Pampas (British, 5415 GRT, built 1941) and Talabot (British, 6798 GRT, built 1936). Close escort was provided by the AA cruiser HMS Carlisle (Capt. D.M.L. Neame, DSO, RN) and the destroyers HMS Sikh (Capt. St.J.A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Zulu (Cdr. H.R. Graham, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Hasty (Lt.Cdr. N.H.G. Austen, RN), HMS Havock (Lt.Cdr. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) and HMS Hero (Cdr. H.W. Biggs, DSO and Bar, RN).

Cover for this convoy was provided by Force B, made up of the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. G. Grantham, DSO, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Dido (Capt. H.W.U. McCall, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN) and the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. J.H. Alliston, DSO, RN), HMS Kingston (Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St.Clair Ford, DSO, RN). This cover force departed Alexandria around 1800 B/20.

At daylight on 21 March the convoy escort was reinfored by the escort destroyers HMS Avon Vale (Lt.Cdr. P.A.R. Withers, DSO, RN), HMS Dulverton (Lt.Cdr. W.N. Petch, OBE, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSC, RN), HMS Hurworth (Lt.Cdr. J.T.B. Birch, RN) and HMS Southwold (Cdr. C.T. Jellicoe, DSC, RN). These escort destroyers had departed Alexandria already on the 19th to proceed to Tobruk while conducting an A/S sweep and fuel there before joining the convoy. A sixth escort destroyer joined later as she had been delayed at Tobruk with a fouled propeller. This was HMS Beaufort (Lt.Cdr. S.O’G Roche, RN) and she sailed from Tobruk around 0945 B/21. Another escort destroyer, HMS Heythrop (Lt.Cdr R.S. Stafford, RN), was torpedoed at 1100 B/20 in position 32°22'N, 25°28'E by the German submarine U-652 while the escort destroyers were conducting their A/S sweep. The stricken ship was taken in tow towards Tobruk by HMS Eridge but she sank at 1600 B/20 in position 32°13'N, 25°33'E.
Shortly after the escort destroyers had joined the convoy escort, Force B made contact with the convoy. The fleet destroyers that had been escorting the convoy the joined that force.

After dark on March 21st, around 2015 B/21, the light cruiser HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyer HMS Legion (Cdr. R.F. Jessel, DSC, RN) departed Malta and joined Force B in the morning of March 22nd.
In a signal timed 0131 B/22, the submarine HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) reported enemy heavy ships leaving Taranto.

In the forenoon light enemy air attacks commenced on the convoy. These developed into heavy air attacks in the afternoon. A total of about 150 enemy aircraft were counted attacking. They concentrated mainly on the convoy but no ships were hit.
At 1430 B/22 Force B sighted four enemy cruisers to the north-east in position 33°53'N, 17°47'E. These were then driven off.

At 1640 B/22 Force B sighted a Littorio-class battleship, two heavy cruisers and some destroyers to the northward. A delaying action was then fought by the cruisers and destroyers of Force B making full use of smoke while the convoy continued on to the westward. Destroyers pressed home a torpedo attack to 6000 yards and reported a hit on the battleship (this was not the case). The battleship was also hit by gunfire and was seen to be on fire aft. One enemy cruiser was seriously damaged and the other was also hit. HMS Cleopatra was hit on the bridge but only sustained minor damage. HMS Kingston was hit in an engine room and HMS Havock in a boiler room. Both destroyers had their speed reduced to 15 knots. HMS Lively was hit forward but was not seriously damaged.

At 1900 B/22 (dusk) the enemy, who had never got within range of the convoy, withdrew to the northward while the convoy was dispersed to Malta with the escorts proceeding with the individual ships. HMS Penelope, HMS Havock, HMS Kingston and HMS Legion were also detached to Malta. Force B set course to Alexandria.

The Italian ships encountered were the following; battleship Vittorio Veneto, heavy cruisers Bolzano, Trento, light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere and the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Fuciliere, Lanciere, Ascari, Aviere, Geniere, Grecale, Alfredo Oriani and Scirocco.

From daylight on March 23rd the ships of the convoy were again subjected to heavy air attacks as they were approaching Malta. At 1040 B/23 Clan Campbell was bombed and sunk in position 35°33'N, 14°35'E. HMS Eridge rescued 113 men. Breconshire was hit in the engine room at 1030 B/23 wen about eight miles from Grand Harbour. She was disabled and attempts by HMS Penelope to take her in tow failed. She drifted towards the shore and came to anchor. Owning to the gale and heavy swell attempts to tow her had to be abandoned.
Pampas and Talabot arrived in harbour safely. HMS Legion was hit but reached Marsaxlokk Harbour and anchored in shallow water.

HMS Carlisle and the Hunt class escort destroyers remained at Malta to provide AA protection for Breconshire. HMS Avon Vale was damaged when she collided with Breconshire and by a near miss. She was unseaworthy. The damaged HMS Kingston and HMS Havock were able to reach Malta safely.

Force B, on their passage east, were delayed by heavy weather. Some of the destroyers sustaining weather damage. The force was bombed during the day but no ship sustained any damage due to the bombing.
An aircraft search for the Italian fleet failed to find any enemy ships.

Shortly after noon on the 24th HMS Cleopatra, HMS Dido, HMS Euryalus, HMS Sikh, HMS Zulu, HMS Hasty, HMS Hero, HMS Jervis, HMS Kelvin and HMS Kipling entered harbour at Alexandria.
