All ships had sustained some form of weather damage. HMS Lively arrived later due to flooding forward. HMS Zulu and HMS Lively were out of action for some weeks.

Meanwhile at Malta weather was still unsuitable to tow Breconshire into the harbour. HMS Southwold was mined while operating near her. She sank while under tow to the harbour. Breconshire was finally towed into the harbour in the morning of the 25th. (24)
22 Mar 1942 At 0050 B/22, in position 40°08'N, 17°06'E, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) heard HE bearing 355°. Nothing yet could be seen in the very dark night.
By 0115 B/22, the HE had drawn round to bearing 325°.
At 0119 B/22, a destroyer was seen approaching. P 36 dived to evade.
At 0125 B/22, two destroyers passed, one on each side of P 36.
At 0129 B/22, a third destroyer passed overhead.
At 0131 B/22, the HE of heavy ships was heard on bearing 020° drawing rapidly to the right.
At 0140 B/22, HMS P 36 surfaced to find nothing in sight.
At 0310 B/22, HMS P 36 started to pass her signal timed 0131 B/22 by W/T in which she reported three destroyers and heavier ships leaving Taranto.
At 0316 B/22, in position 40°05'N, 17°04'E, HE of heavy ships was heard on bearing 360°. P 36 immediately stopped transmitting.
By 0321 B/22, the HE had drawn from right to left, to 350°, then started to draw from left to right. It was considered the ships would pass close to P 36.
At 0330 B/22, the HE bore 050°. It was estimated that P 36 was on the beam of the enemy ships, but nothing was sighted.

At 0400 B/22, the signal timed 0131 B/22 was successfully passed by W/T. The signal contained the following information; Position, course and speed of three destroyers and the HE of heavier ships, 40°08'N, 17°07'E, 150°, 23 knots.
The following Italian Fleet units had departed Taranto earlier;

Group 1: The destroyer Aviere sailed at 2310 B/21, the destroyer Ascari at 2326 B/21, the destroyer Alfredo Oriani at 2330 B/21, the destroyer Grecale at 2330 B/21 and the battleship Littorio sailed at 2351 B/21.
Group 2: The destroyer Geniere sailed at 0158 B/22 and the destroyer Scirocco sailed at 0250 B/22. (25)

22 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2339 B/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Establish patrol as follows by noon/23 P 36 in position 'R', 40°19'N, 17°06'E. Upholder in position 'S', 40°12'N, 17°03'E. Proteus in position 'T', 40°08'N, 17°08'E. Enemy force including damaged battleship expected return Taranto on mean course 340° to 40°18'N, 17°03'N, thence 040°. If A/S measures force retirement, P 36 keep north of 40°15'N and Upholder and Proteus south of that latitude, former west and latter east of 17°05'E. (23)
23 Mar 1942 At 1405 B/23, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) heard the HE of a destroyer.
At 1408 B/23, a Navigatori-class destroyer was seen steering 200° in approximate position 40°16'N, 17°09'E.
This was Antonio Pigafetta which sailed from Taranto at 1205 B/23 to reinforce the escort of the battleship Littorio returning to Taranto. She returned at 1955 B/23.
At 1700 B/23, HE was heard bearing 180°. This was thought to be originating from possibly two destroyers but nothing was sighted.
At 1730 B/23, the HE faded out on bearing 250°.
At 2030 B/23, HMS P 36 surfaced and a fix taken at 2200 B/23 showed that she had been about five miles to the south-south-east of position 'R' (40°19'N, 17°06'E) during the day.

At 2337 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2229 B/23 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 36; Leave the vicinity of Cape Colonne P.M./24 through position 37°35'N, 16°45'E to arrive at Malta A.M./27. (25)

24 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1451 B/24, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Return forthwith to patrol positions as given in my signal timed 2339 B/22; Proteus keep north-east and P 36 south-west of a line 160° from position 'R'. Enemy unit still at sea. (23)
25 Mar 1942 At 0425 B/25, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) arrived back in position 'R' (40°19'N, 17°06'E). She then dived.
At 0715 B/25, two trawler type ships were sighted leaving Taranto. They were transmitting on Asdic.
At 1030 B/25, the same two vessels were seen to the south, proceeding westwards.
At 1709 B/25, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1355 B/25 was received which contained the following orders; Return by route in my signal timed 2229 B/23 to arrive at Malta 1930 B/28 or A/M./29.
At 1834 B/25, a destroyer was seen approaching Taranto at 20 knots.
This was the torpedo boat Antares which sailed from Taranto at 1010 B/25 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1935 B/25.
At 1840 B/25, the two trawlers were seen again. They disappeared to the westward and returned as it got dark which delayed HMS P 36 surfacing.

At 2139 B/25, HMS P 36 surfaced. The two trawlers were seen again on bearing 050° at a range of about 3 to 4 miles. The could not see the submarine against the dark, cloudy horizon nor did they hear her surface. Course was set for Cape Colonne and then to Malta.
[We have been unable to identify the two trawlers that had been sighted several times throughout the day.] (16)
29 Mar 1942 At 0945 B/29, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
1 Apr 1942 Around 1430 B/1, while alongside HMS Talbot, HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) is bombed and sunk during an Axis air raid on Malta, there were no casualties.

The submarine was shut with watertight doors closed and one officer and two ratings in the control room. The bomb went through the catamaran on the port side and exploded, blowing in the port side of the control room. The 3 men onboard miraculously escaped. The submarine sank and rolled over into deep water. (16)
